title: Why the 2s State Lives a Hundred Million Times Longer Than It Should

metadata.color_law: TEAL = allowed transitions (solid arrows, nanosecond clock); CRIMSON = the forbidden 2s->1s gap (dashed arrow, slow clock)
metadata.note: Exclusions: no electric-dipole matrix element derivation, no quantum electrodynamics, no full Balmer/Lyman series survey, no antihydrogen CPT comparison

swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Excited hydrogen atoms decay in nanoseconds. The 2s state takes a tenth of a second — a hundred million times longer. The energy is there. The destination is there. Something is blocking it.

card.title: Why the 2s State Lives a Hundred Million Times Longer Than It Should

beats.act: COLD OPEN — normal decays happen fast
beats.narration_text: Most excited hydrogen states decay in a few nanoseconds. Raise an electron to 2p, 3s, or 3d — it drops almost instantly, releasing a photon. Every allowed transition is over in a billionth of a second.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Most excited hydrogen states decay in a few nanoseconds. Raise an electron to 2p, 3s, or 3d — it drops almost instantly, releasing a photon. Every allowed trans

beats.narration_text: The 2s state is different. It sits at the same energy as 2p, with the same 10.2 eV to shed. But it waits 0.12 seconds before emitting — a hundred million times longer than its neighbors.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The 2s state is different. It sits at the same energy as 2p, with the same 10.2 eV to shed. But it waits 0.12 seconds before emitting — a hundred million times

beats.narration_text: The 2s state has 10.2 eV of energy it could shed by dropping to 1s. Other levels at that energy decay in nanoseconds. Why can't the 2s state go?

card.title: The 2s state has 10.2 eV and a clear destination. Why can't it decay?

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The 2s state has 10.2 eV of energy it could shed by dropping to 1s. Other levels at that energy decay in nanoseconds. Why can't the 2s state go?

beats.act: THE PROBLEM — naive picture: energy gap should be enough
beats.narration_text: The naive picture says any state with a gap below it should decay. The gap exists. The 1s state is right there. The 2p state makes the same jump in nanoseconds. Why does angular momentum matter?

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The naive picture says any state with a gap below it should decay. The gap exists. The 1s state is right there. The 2p state makes the same jump in nanoseconds.

beats.act: THE MECHANISM — a photon carries angular momentum
beats.narration_text: Here is the constraint. A single photon carries exactly one unit of angular momentum. When an atom emits a photon, angular momentum must be conserved. The electron's orbital angular momentum must change by one unit.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Here is the constraint. A single photon carries exactly one unit of angular momentum. When an atom emits a photon, angular momentum must be conserved. The elect

beats.act: THE MECHANISM — delta ell = 0 blocks the transition
beats.narration_text: The 2s state has orbital angular momentum zero — ell equals zero. The 1s state also has ell equals zero. The change in ell is zero. But the photon must carry away ell equals one. That balance is impossible. The transition is forbidden by an exact conservation law.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The 2s state has orbital angular momentum zero — ell equals zero. The 1s state also has ell equals zero. The change in ell is zero. But the photon must carry aw

beats.act: THE MECHANISM — why 2p can go and 2s cannot
beats.narration_text: The 2p state has ell equals one. It drops to 1s with ell equals zero. Change: minus one. The photon carries away that one unit of angular momentum. Conservation holds. The 2p decay is allowed. The 2s decay is not.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The 2p state has ell equals one. It drops to 1s with ell equals zero. Change: minus one. The photon carries away that one unit of angular momentum. Conservation

beats.act: THE IMPLICATION — the two-photon escape route
beats.narration_text: The 2s state is not completely stuck. Two photons can share the angular momentum between them — each carrying a fraction, totaling one unit. That route is allowed but rare, which is why the lifetime stretches to 0.12 seconds.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The 2s state is not completely stuck. Two photons can share the angular momentum between them — each carrying a fraction, totaling one unit. That route is allow

beats.narration_text: That long lifetime makes the 2s state metastable — stable enough to interrogate with a laser pulse. Physicists use the 1s-2s two-photon transition to measure hydrogen to fifteen significant figures. The forbidden rule is what makes the measurement possible.

beats.narration_text: A student prepares a beam of hydrogen atoms all excited to 2s. She expects them to decay quickly — energy gap, nearby destination. After a tenth of a second, most still haven't decayed. She traces the rule: ell equals zero to ell equals zero, change is zero, photon needs one unit. The conservation law explains every surviving atom in her beam.

beats.narration_text: A photon carries one unit of angular momentum. The 2s and 1s states both have zero angular momentum. Their difference is zero — but the photon needs one. That mismatch forbids the decay. Angular momentum conservation, not weak coupling, is what makes the 2s state live a hundred million times longer.

card.title: The 2s->1s decay is forbidden by angular momentum conservation.
